Day 1 - Learning ThinkPHP From Scratch: An Introduction

ThinkPHP is a fairly uncommon framework. I picked it as a personal challenge because I'd actually written with it before (and gotten beaten up by it). Back then I was on version 3.2.4, but the current version is 5.1, and honestly ThinkPHP changed a ton in between — even the system architecture is wildly different. So enough rambling, let's start learning ThinkPHP 5.1 from the basics.
The version of ThinkPHP I'd touched before was 3.2.4, and my takeaway after building with it was that it's a really beginner-friendly framework. But some of the syntax was honestly
terse, which caused a lot of headaches during development. For example, calling across controllers looked like
. If you didn't have the developer manual open, you'd have no clue what kind of magic
actually were. That said, once you read through the official developer manual, building a website with it is no problem at all.

Later, to make the system support PHP 7.0 and up, the ThinkPHP team did a big overhaul of the whole architecture in ThinkPHP 5. Personally, I think if you've done a bit of Laravel, you'll find it kind of familiar (the core is modeled after Laravel). The current LTS release is 5.1 — and yes, the latest version is 6.0 (I hear 6.0 got reworked again, but I haven't dug into it). 6.0 still has a few rough edges, though, so this series is going to focus on ThinkPHP 5.1.

And the team also provides a complete ThinkPHP developer manual (in Chinese!). If your English is as rough as mine, this framework might be worth a shot. Basically, once you've learned one framework, going to look at another one, you'll notice a surprising amount of overlap.
ThinkPHP 5.1 requires PHP 5.6 or higher, so if you're below 5.6 you won't be able to use it.
This series installs ThinkPHP 5.1 via Composer.
Because XAMPP ships with MariaDB built in.
Here's my own dev setup for reference.
One tiny but sneaky gotcha to add: if you have VMware installed on your machine, remember to open Task Manager and stop

, otherwise you might run into the lovely situation where Apache refuses to start. The nice thing about developing with XAMPP is that error messages get listed right below, which speeds up troubleshooting ↓

Since we're going to learn a framework starting from basic SQL, we'll go from basic SQL syntax all the way to using the ThinkPHP framework. Of course, this series might not follow a strict order — I'll mix in some basics here and there, so you can see the difference between raw syntax and the helper libraries the framework provides.
I'd also recommend having some basic programming background for this series. As long as you know
, you should be more or less fine.
